question: The slide showing the subtree augmentation section lists node.size = node.left.size + node.right.size + 1. How does this equation connect to the lecture's explanation of why a recursive size function would be inefficient?
answer: The slide equation node.size = node.left.size + node.right.size + 1 represents a stored, precomputed value rather than a live recursive computation. The lecture explains that if size were called as a recursive function, it would take linear time because it would traverse all nodes in the subtree. Instead, by storing the size directly on each node and updating it in O(1) per node by applying this update rule bottom-up through ancestors after any insertion or deletion, the size query becomes O(1) constant time — just return node.size.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing node.size = node.left.size + node.right.size + 1 on the subtree augmentation slide
Explanation that recursive size function gives linear time and why precomputing stored size is needed

question: The slide on subtree augmentation shows 'subtree property can be computed from properties of node's children (and node) in O(1) time'. How does this relate to the lecture's explanation of why depth cannot be maintained as an augmentation property?
answer: The slide defines a subtree property as one that can be computed from a node's own children's properties in O(1) time — it is inherently downward-looking within the subtree. Depth, however, depends on how many ancestors a node has above it, which is information from outside the subtree. Specifically, a node's depth depends on the length of the path from the root to that node, which involves nodes not in the node's own subtree. Since depth cannot be computed knowing only the node's children's properties, it violates the subtree property definition and cannot be efficiently maintained as an augmentation.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing subtree augmentation definition: property can be computed from node's children in O(1) time
Explanation that depth is not a subtree property because it depends on nodes outside the subtree

question: The slide showing the sequence binary tree application lists the subtree_at(node, i) algorithm. How does this algorithm differ from the subtree_find algorithm shown on the set binary tree slide, and why is that difference necessary?
answer: The set binary tree slide shows subtree_find(node, k) which branches based on comparing a search key k to node.item.key — it uses the actual key values stored in items. The sequence binary tree slide shows subtree_at(node, i) which branches based on comparing index i to nL = size(node.left) — it uses subtree sizes rather than item keys. This difference is necessary because sequence binary trees do not require items to have keys or any ordering property; instead, position is determined structurally by counting nodes in each subtree, allowing arbitrary sequences to be maintained and searched by index.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing subtree_find(node, k) algorithm for set binary tree using key comparisons
Frame caption showing subtree_at(node, i) algorithm for sequence binary tree using nL = size(node.left)

question: What does the slide or frame caption show as the title and list of topics covered in today's lecture on Binary Trees II?
answer: The slide shows 'Today: Binary Trees II: AVL' as the title, and lists the following topics: subtree augmentation, sequence binary tree via subtree sizes, height balance (AVL) via height augmentation, rotations, rebalancing, and sorting.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing the lecture slide titled 'Today: Binary Trees II: AVL' with the list of topics

question: How does the lecture's proof that height-balanced AVL trees have O(log n) height use the recurrence for minimum nodes, and what simplification is applied to make the recurrence tractable?
answer: The lecture constructs the worst-case height-balanced tree where every node has skew of exactly one, giving a recurrence N_h = N_{h-1} + N_{h-2} + 1 for the minimum number of nodes at height h. To make this tractable, the lecture drops the plus-one and replaces N_{h-1} with N_{h-2} — valid because N_{h-1} >= N_{h-2} by induction — yielding N_h >= 2 * N_{h-2}. This recurrence solves to N_h >= 2^(h/2), which is exponential in h, so inverting shows h <= 2 * log n, meaning height is always O(log n), within a factor of two of the ideal.

ground_truth_spans.description: Construction of worst-case AVL tree and statement of recurrence N_h = N_{h-1} + N_{h-2} + 1
Simplification replacing N_{h-1} with N_{h-2} to get N_h >= 2*N_{h-2} and solving to exponential bound

question: What is the specific update rule for maintaining height as a subtree augmentation in an AVL tree, and why does this make height a valid subtree property?
answer: The height update rule is: node.height = 1 + max(node.left.height, node.right.height), which computes the height of a node by taking the maximum of the heights of its left and right children and adding one to count the edge to the deeper child. This makes height a valid subtree property because to compute or update the height of any node you need only the already-stored height values of its two direct children — no other information from anywhere else in the tree is needed — and the computation takes O(1) time, satisfying the definition of a subtree property exactly.

ground_truth_spans.description: Explanation of height update rule as max of left and right child heights plus one, and why this is a subtree property

question: According to the lecture slide shown on screen, what topics are covered under 'Today: Binary Trees II: AVL', and how does the traversal order concept introduced in the recall section connect to the sequence binary tree application discussed later?
answer: The slide lists six topics under 'Today: Binary Trees II: AVL': subtree augmentation, sequence binary tree via subtree sizes, height balance (AVL) via height augmentation, rotations, rebalancing, and sorting. The recall section defines traversal order as recursively traversing the left subtree, then outputting the root, then traversing the right subtree. This traversal order directly defines the sequence order maintained by a sequence binary tree, meaning the traversal order of the tree is set to equal the sequence order the programmer wants to represent.

ground_truth_spans.description: Slide caption listing today's topics including subtree augmentation, sequence binary tree via subtree sizes, height balance AVL, rotations, rebalancing, sorting
Explanation that sequence binary tree sets traversal order equal to sequence order

question: The lecture slide shows the subtree_find algorithm for a set binary tree, and the spoken explanation connects it to binary search on arrays. What is the BST property shown on the slide, and how does the lecturer describe the relationship between subtree_find and binary search on arrays?
answer: The slide shows the BST property as: keys in subtree(node.left) < node.item.key < keys in subtree(node.right). The lecturer explains that this algorithm is exactly binary search on an array: binary search first looks at the key in the middle (drawn as the root), then recurses either on the left chunk or the right chunk, so a perfect binary tree simulates exactly binary search in an array. The lecturer calls set binary trees 'binary search trees' because they are the tree version of binary search.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing BST property and subtree_find algorithm on slide
Lecturer explains subtree_find generalizes binary search from arrays to trees, calls set binary trees binary search trees

question: The frame caption for the subtree augmentation section shows the equation node.size = node.left.size + node.right.size + 1. How does the lecturer justify storing this value on every node rather than computing it recursively, and how many ancestor nodes must be updated when a leaf is added?
answer: The lecturer explains that computing size recursively as a function call would give a linear-time algorithm, which would make the overall operations very slow. Instead, size is stored on every single node and precomputed, so the function simply returns node.size in constant time. When a leaf is added, only the ancestors of that new node need their size updated, which the lecturer says is order H ancestors (approximately exactly H), since subtrees not containing the new leaf are unchanged.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecturer explains storing size on every node rather than recursive computation, frame caption shows node.size equation
Lecturer explains that adding a leaf requires updating order H ancestors up the tree

question: The frame captions show a slide listing 'subtree augmentation' with the rule that each node can store O(1) extra fields, and later a slide showing the subtree_at algorithm. What index adjustment does the subtree_at algorithm make when recursing into the right subtree, and why is this necessary according to the lecture?
answer: When recursing into the right subtree, the subtree_at algorithm subtracts nL and 1 from the index i, computing i minus nL minus 1. This is necessary because the numbering system changes when moving into the right subtree: in the global tree, index zero is the leftmost node, but within the right subtree, index zero refers to the first node of that subtree. The root node itself occupies index nL, so the right subtree's local index must subtract both nL (the left subtree count) and 1 (for the root node).

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows subtree_at algorithm with nL computation and recursion cases
Lecturer explains the subtraction i-nL-1 when recursing right, because numbering resets in the right subtree

question: A frame caption shows the AVL height balance condition as height(node.right) - height(node.left) in {-1, 0, +1}. The lecturer then proves that height-balanced trees have logarithmic height using a recurrence. What recurrence does the lecturer set up and what is the final bound on height H in terms of n?
answer: The lecturer defines N_h as the minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree of height H. In the worst case, one subtree has height H-1 and the other has height H-2, giving the recurrence N_h = N_{h-1} + N_{h-2} + 1. The lecturer then lower-bounds this by replacing N_{h-1} with N_{h-2} and dropping the +1, giving N_h ≥ 2 * N_{h-2}, which solves to approximately 2^(h/2). Inverting this exponential relationship gives H ≤ 2 * log n, so AVL trees always have height at most 2 log n.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows AVL height balance condition skew in {-1,0,+1} and lecturer sets up recurrence N_h = N_{h-1} + N_{h-2} + 1
Lecturer solves recurrence to show N_h is exponential and concludes H is at most 2 log n

question: The frame caption for the subtree augmentation section shows 'each node can store O(1) extra fields/properties' and the height update rule. How does the lecturer use height as a subtree property, and what happens to the augmentation of nodes X and Y specifically after a rotation is performed?
answer: Height is a subtree property because the height of a node equals the maximum of the heights of its left and right children plus one, which can be computed in constant time from the children's stored heights. After a rotation, the subtrees A, B, and C do not change, so their augmentations are unaffected. However, X's subtree changes because X now has Y as a child (or vice versa), so X's augmentation must be updated. Then Y's augmentation must also be updated. Finally, all ancestors of X must be updated walking up the tree.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecturer explains height is a subtree property computed as max of children heights plus one
Lecturer explains after rotation X and Y augmentations must be updated, then all ancestors

question: What does the frame caption for the sequence binary tree application show as the code for subtree_at(node, i)?
answer: The frame caption shows the function subtree_at(node, i) with the following steps: nL = size(node.left); if i < nL, call Subtree_at(node.left, i); if i = nL, return node; if i > nL, call Subtree_at(node.right, i - nL). The diagram accompanying the code depicts a binary tree node with left and right subtrees.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption explicitly showing subtree_at code with nL = size(node.left) and three cases

question: What does the frame caption show as the content written on the blackboard regarding 'Subtree augmentation' rules?
answer: The frame caption shows three bullet points written on the blackboard under 'Subtree augmentation': each node can store O(1) extra fields/properties; subtree property can be computed from properties of node's children (and node) in O(1) time; and node.size = node.left.size + node.right.size + 1. A diagram of two connected nodes labeled P is also shown to the right of the text.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing blackboard text listing the three subtree augmentation rules including node.size equation

question: Why can the global index of a node not be maintained as a subtree augmentation property, and why can depth also not be maintained as a subtree property according to the lecture?
answer: The global index cannot be maintained as a subtree property because if a new node is inserted at the beginning of the traversal order, every node's index changes. This means one change causes all properties to change simultaneously, which is an extreme example of a global property rather than a local one. The lecturer notes that a node's index depends on how many nodes are to its left (its predecessors), which are not in the subtree of that node. Depth is also mentioned as annoying to maintain, though the lecturer says it is not obvious why at that moment.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecturer explains index cannot be maintained because inserting at beginning changes all indices
Lecturer explains index depends on nodes to the left which are outside the node's subtree, and mentions depth is also not maintainable

question: What does the lecturer say is the goal of today's lecture in terms of improving the time complexity of binary tree operations?
answer: The goal of today's lecture is to guarantee that the tree height H equals O(log n), thereby transforming all operations that currently run in O(H) time so that they run in O(log n) time. The lecturer says this is achieved by modifying the data structure already seen, without a lot of additional work, using something called AVL trees or height balance.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecturer states the goal is to guarantee H is log n so all O(H) operations become O(log n), achieved via AVL trees
